author: Huy
content:
As many of you have already agreed on this - no more 0's! No ifs. The trade has to work out right away and the move has to be impulsive for 0's to work in our favor. Also it takes another level of mental capital to be able to hold through the ups and downs. Only Jack and Tony can get away with this due to where they are in their trading journey + their experience. My suggestion is only take 0's once you get to a certain point but the size is going to be much smaller. Vast majority of us including myself are not there yet.

I can give you 2 feedbacks on the trade today. 
1. NVDA was consolidating after we got in but it never broke market structure. There was an order block that it was respecting nicely
2. QQQ also never broke structure

do not get me wrong, today was also tough. It takes some mental fortitude to hold through that

author: 305 Trader
content: one of the secrets of staying alive as an Options Trader, is to lose small. So I share with you a trick from observation. When you take an entry and the stock moves in your direction but wicks out a lot, this means there is still indecision. Do not get caught in that fight. Exit at small green or small red and wait for conviction from your Buyers. You can always reenter, if you were correct the market will go the way you needed and if it doesnt you were out already.

author: Henry
content:
Hello everyone, I’ve been on this forum for six or seven months now. I’ve figured out why I can identify every single trade made by Tony and Jack, and I’m able to follow their moves accordingly. Someone told me that Hyenuk the Korean/Colombian trader who teaches in Spanish applies the rule of 8% of his contracts size for everything, either swings or intraday. Never forget this, you can always re-enter. It took me almost 4 years to understand and apply this simple notion to become profitable. Sometimes, the obvious things are realized late and very very costly.

author: 305 Trader
content: Here, this is how I learned it from both Jack and Tony. Now choose three colors only for horizontal lines, in this case there are more because of the ATHs levels we are having, but typically I use:  Red for PDsH (Supply area is usually red), Green for PDsL (demand area is usually green). Tony uses it inverted but I think is more accurate Red = Top = Supply  and Green = Botom = Demand. Then I use a third color to identify a strong zone of multiple points of confluence over time whether on the D chart but mostly on the 1H TF found, and then I proceed to connect those horizontal lines with a relevant area of confluence on a lower TF  for me being the 10 mins TF, but you can use the 15 mins  TF. This is the most important part of your morning routine in preparation for the market opening, identifying where those areas of liquidity are there.

content:
for me the entry point was was also good BUT only the timing was off and this can be a huge downside. So here is the entry that would have been optimal for me. It's the exact same SL/TP only with a slightly earlier entry. During the same time QQQ was also moving up after creating a potential HL in the 1min.
https://www.tradingview.com/x/BURYd40B/

Earlier entry also means that the SL is lower. In this case the SL would have been just below the 5min range (so a reasonable SL compared to the first screenshot). No candle close below the SL and therefore this entry would have worked.
Such a "small" detail but in this case this led to a loosing day for me instead of a winning day. In options trading sometimes just a few seconds can make a huge difference. This is just another example for me that I need to be fast on my entries and avoid entries that are too late / too far away from the level. author: 305 Trader
content: Good morning. You were correct, the bottom was near but not quite at the time of your entry. It was at your exit.  Go in higher Time Frames, 4H and 1H with extended hours and find zones of solid confluence and draw them as rectangles as Jack does. Polish them in 15 mins TF around their edges. They will indicate bottoms and tops for larger moves. You need only few, maybe three max, 4. When the stock breaks those levels with a retest and making new higher lows (long) or new Lower highs (short) prior your entry, plus increasing volume and a solid reaction ( a hammer or a solid large bar),its a confirmation the reversal  or continuation is in place. This is how big moves are caught. If you only wait for this and do nothing, absolutely nothing else in your trading, you will transform your account. Then, study, study off time how the best break outs happen out of those levels and refine your entry. Go back in time in your Time frame of preference and one higher to study these breakouts from these zones you drew your zones exactly as I mentioned and study how the breakout took shape and what is the perfect set up for your entry. Last one, take this model and specialize in only one stock. Once you have dominated this (with $$$ to demonstrate it), you can add more stocks to your watch list. Of course, we often do what we want and think is correct, but if you would just listen to this advice, I promise you, you can get rich. This is exactly what I am doing now.

author: Humantorch
content: If you do marker order, I've been there myself many times. It's a mental game as much as it is technical. Focus is a big part of it. Once you see price starts moving to your TP, you have to get ready with the finger on a trigger. Sometimes the move happens slowly and you have lots of time to close it. Other times there are pops in price and you have to be fast to close your position otherwise it MAY reverse completely and you don't get a chance to close it, wait for maybe another chance and it goes red and you end up losing the trade.

I hear you, but have to also say - try and keep it as simple as possible at first and let the options contract do what it does. Otherwise it’s easy to get caught managing the cons instead of the trade idea.

 Fwiw, I only ever use market orders on my sells, unless I'm trading something super spready (MU, META etc)  

There’s always going to be a little imperfection with fills/slippage but consistency in your own execution process is what matters, more than trying to optimize every exit perfectly

author: Kam
content:
Yeah that’s one of the harder parts with options. The cons don't always move with the underlying especially on fast moves, wide spreads, or later in the week. I have rules to size down on Thu, Fri even tho cons look cheaper

Another idea is to think  in “risk bands”, e.g' if your intention is to risk $100, accept that your real risk is likely $100-$175 depending on the contract dynamics

It’s never perfectly precise with options unfortunately, part of the business. We definitely all lose more than intended on those kill candles when they happen though. But with the right tactics you can put up some safeguards

author: nasman
content:
Hey everyone, wanted to share a transparent update. I’ve officially wiped the $1k I started with.

This wasn't a failure of the strategy or the course, it was 100% my execution. My journal shows a "death by a thousand cuts" from:

- FOMO & Chasing: Entering late and forcing setups.
- IBKR slips / accidental execution
- Revenge Trading: Trying to "claw back" small losses and emotionally reacting.
- Stop Loss Management: I honored my stops, but didn't adjust/edit them properly for the price action.

Fortunately I only traded one contract and kept my position size small at all times. 

I’m stepping back to reset and build back the right way while trying to refine my edge. 

For the mentors and experienced traders:
1.    How do you mentally reset after a wipeout to avoid a "deficit mindset" on the next trade? I do see myself holding biases from losses. 

2.    What’s your best drill for rebuilding entry discipline?

Appreciate any feedback. Back to work!

author: Kam
content:
Hey there, 

The fact that you can clearly identify the actual behaviors so specifically is probably more important long term than the $1k itself.

For me personally, the biggest reset after rough patches has always been reducing the pressure to “make it back” and just getting laser focused on execution - especially if I know execution was the issue (vs. market or normal variance). I usually size down until I see that I'm trading my process / setups consistently again. 

Regarding drills - I think this depends on each person to a certain extent, but something that helped reduce my trade count was really focusing on my filters for (dis)qualifying a setup. If you try and actively ask yourself 'why not' when considering a trade it can help with awareness & logic vs just getting locked in on the patterns we've been trained to recognize in the charts. 

Hope that helps - love the attitude 💪

The only answer is: keep trying to figure it out for your self. But, with only 1x con. No more. Forget about the notion of getting rich or making a living out of this. Just sit down and trade with just 1x con, adjust your entries. The market has turned difficult for the day trader (not the swing trader with capital to hold). Corrections are very brief, ranges are long for some symbols. so go long for the most part. I also had a bad probably worst month of the year in May. Also, because I departed from what made me profitable in 2026 to try a new strategy which proved it is not working in this market with the Elliott waves I was trying. So, what we have to do is to adhere to a set of 3-4 rules and pick up our brains from the garbage can and force it to sit, “just to sit”and to wait for the rules to present themselves in the form of a set up and hit it. But also to act early as soon as they are spotted, this is the flow, to be able to cruise in low speed for a while but to switch gear quickly when the rules present themselves or viceversa. There it lies the fluidity of the same day trader.

author: 305 Trader
content: you are missing a key element, in your analysis. Its the HTF. In the 1H TF and 4H Tf identify strong levels of support and resistance (often are pivot points), and plan your trades on the reaction to those levels, as well as use them to identify your exits. That move most certainly in AMD hit an important support level at 486 in 1H Tf. Take a look and you may see this. Do not trade if you dont make a thesis based on laying out a plan on these two HTF.

It seems that "we" were taking INTC again today (and I can clearly see the INTC trade on the INTC chart), but I do not see support for any Long trade on the QQQ 1m TF at the time of the INTC trade.
QQQ was below PDH, below PML, breaking below the 5mOR, pulling back on the hourly (when you include after-hours). I do see Buyers stepping in on the 9:46 candle, but then an immediate inverted hammer on the 9:47.

I am trying to focus on only trading one symbol, QQQ. You can see that I waited patiently until the PDH B&R. It broke... put in a hammer, and then I entered looking for PWH. It came back and tested PDH again (and the Option price actually went below my Stop). Then came back up again, reached HoD, but was not a 2:1, so did not sell. Then I got Stopped Out.
I considered the second area, but noticed Sellers kept pushing it down, and then noticed the purple resistance line. I called it quits on another red day.

So, can anyone help me understand how QQQ supported the INTC (and AMD) trade when it was heading south and below all the levels on the 1m TF ?
And, what was wrong with my QQQ trade idea ?

author: 305 Trader
content: I think you are not working with the higher Time Frames: 4H and 1H, you seem to be taken only entries based on a B&R to a level. Try to understand what your stock is doing in: Daily, 4H and 1H. Find relevant key zones from there. Watch those videos where the guys teach this and then, study the reactions to those levels and what makes a good entry. In essence, start from zero this weekend.

author: 305 Trader
content: Ok <@1490749230837399662> thanks for sharing. This is what I think it happened against you. - You did not allow a solid retest to the PDH to occur. This is a must at a very important key level such as PDH. If by waiting so the move takes off w/o you so be it. 2nd, Even if you waited for a solid retest to PDH as it happened few mins later in your chart, the PMH which was still not revisited by then, was quite close to your entry. So that dwindled further your shot at target of 1:2, for at best that entry was a 1:2 with zero error for the buyers. Wait up for larger space in your potential run, it will give you higher probs and also do not rush the retest, it has to be noticeable as per Hayden's explanations.

author: Torr
content: Scale down, even to a single share and just get some wins. You'll feel better. Just don't scale too quick.

author: Torr
content:
The way I've managed this is two loses, I scale down for the day to half risk on everything. If I lose three in a row, I'm done. I only risk .5 R ever, so I only need 1.5 trades to go right to be back to green the next day. If I have a string of losing days (two in a row), I scale down at the start of the next day. Two green days, and I scale up again. My reasoning for this is that I'm losing either because 1. I don't have the edge I think I do (perhaps because market conditions have shifted without me knowing) 2. My head isn't right 3. I'm just hitting a losing streak, which is probalistic certainty in trading. Either of these options mean it's better to just scale back or stop. 

But I also get it that we get hijacked sometimes.

author: Jdub
content: No one knows the exact amount that the  contracts will be worth at a certain price due to the greeks always moving. The reason I said 14 was my risk is becasue if you see on the options contract that was the break of strucutre level. If price closed below the key level the contracts would be worth around 14 ish. In this case it was an a+ setup. I usually risk around $2000 on A+ setups. Lets say I wanted to risk $3500 on a trade where the contracts are worth $1.75 then my stop would be at $1.40 on the contracts.

author: Jdub
content: Yes. When trading options espically right off of the open it can be hard to manage risk sometimes becuase price moves so quick. The easiest way is to look at the options chart real quick to give you an idea of how much your risking. If price is consolidating and you have more time you can calculate the risk amount with delta for a more precise risk amount. That's why when you risk a same amount per trade you understand lets say for example tsla I only need to give it 10% to know if I'm right or wrong. So if I usually risk 10% that means I will risk $750-$1000. That means my posistion size will be anywhere between $7500-10,000 position size depedning on the price of the contracts.

author: 👑 of Puts
content: I'd like to share few things. It's been 2 months in this room. And i had my attention glued to mainly learning. Taken probably about 10-15 trades in 2 months😵‍💫  Could have really followed the call outs and made money  🤌  But i can't take something i don't understand. Here's a few things that really helped: Creating a proper playbook with screenshots and entry/exit criteria; Backtesting each setup (proper backtesting, i realized i did it wrong all those years); doing video trade reviews (same as Tony and Jake do on youtube. created a channel and posting it there) Helps so much! Also created a discord where i post my watchlist and my thoughts. Having my thoughts written out helps with discipline!!! Some days i think i didn't make any progress so i made a list of things i learned in 2 months and it's impressive. Next step is getting off the lives and doing my own stream so i can talk through my thoughts and watch my emotions. Will review posted lives for my mistakes and thought process. My main message here i guess i can't do less than Tony or Jake and be profitable.

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content: I have relevant problems, and I know some other people have these, because it's difficult to manage risk. If you enter with Tony/Jdub, it might be later than them because the video might lag behind. Thus stop loss is wider. Scaling out at HoD makes RR less. This might lead to losing the same amount on losing trades that you gain on winning trades (and no continuation on trailers). If you can't manage to risk each time the equal amount, which is hard because you don't always know the stop loss potential risk when you enter the trade, you might end up red.

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content:
Actually, when to enter and what kind of price action to seek on entry seems to me even harder than finding a working setup. Especially with small scalps to HoD, if you have to enter late, you better skip this trade. 

I rewatched some videos about the strategy recently, and I noticed that sometimes Jdub  enters the trade  upwards on the black candle of retest, without waiting for a green candle to form and show some displacement. Thus the risk is much smaller. That's a thing I want to look into, which entry conditions work better in which kind of trade and market.

Also, during the last month I've noticed (maybe it's just coincidental) that if the trade goes in the wrong direction or doesn't show impulse in the first couple of minutes, most of the times it will eventually exit on stop loss. I'm thinking of cutting the trades that don't seem to work earlier. Of course, there are trades where a wick touches the stop loss and the price goes up and reaches the PT, but doesn't seem to me that there are many of these.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
**RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY **

Seriously, take a screenshot of this and keep it handy. 

If you’re green for the week, only trade with a small **PORTION** of your profits. For example, if you’re up $1,000, risk just $200-$300. Even if you lose it, you’ll still end the week green. 

If you’re green but don’t have enough profits to play with, **DO NOT TRADE**. 

If you’re red for the week, **DO NOT TRADE**. 

Even if you’re green and have profits to spare, you can always take Friday off. Enjoy a 3-day weekend—it’s never a bad idea. 

Remember, Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you. 

Stick to these rules, and you’ll never end the week on a bad note.

Please confirm you have read this with a 👍🏼

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content:
Hi guys, this might be interesting to anyone struggling with RR like I do. I revised my trades (made during live sessions) of the last month, and here's the stats.

There were 10 losing trades (exit on SL, actually 9, but for one trade I couldn't remember the setup so I counted it as a loss in terms of this analysis), and 12 winning trades. 3 of the losses were on FOMC day, so they can actually be excluded as I shouldn't have taken them at all. But still.

RR of the winning trades: 
9,39
4,73
4,07
2,08
3,29
2,05
4,32
3,04
1,4
3,16
5,18
3,3
That's if I didn't close position until the initial stop loss was hit and just waited until the trend changes or the day ends, and took profit at maximum (which you never know for sure, of course).

So there was only one trade with RR less than 2.
I'm actually red this month due to uneven risk management and selling 30-50% of the position at HoD most of the times (I trade 4-6 cons).

So I made these calculations to understand what kind of result I would have now if I only sold at 2RR or 3RR.
Check the screenshot, it's all there. 
If you trade a small number of cons as I do you might come to the same conclusion as I did, that even closing the whole position at 3RR without taking partial scales at HoD/LoD might have been rather profitable.

Of course, that's a random month and these stats aren't really representative. But I personally want to try something like selling 4 cons out of 6 at 2RR, and the rest (2) at 3RR.

Hope this is helpful 🙌

2) RR 0.68 at HoD

4) Never reached HoD 
5) One losing trade I don’t remember the setup so no info on that one

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content:
I never moved my SL to BE, I just scaled out 30-50% at HoD/LoD and scaled out the rest at psychological numbers and final target without any trailers. This led me to playing most of the time at RR 1:1, if not worse. That's why I decided to check the actual potential RR of the trades I took.

As for the analysis, you can see above that of my 9 losing trades I could restore, only 1 could use scaling out at HoD/LoD, and the rest of losing trades never reached HoD/LoD. So, if of my 12 winning trades I scale all of them 50% at HoD/LoD, that's 50% loss of potential profit of 12 trades, compared to this one trade where kinda saved 1/2 of risk by scaling out at HoD/LoD.

Of my trades of this month, many times price was kinda consolidating right after the entry, so closing position at BE didn't make any sense most of the time

Like this trade on AAPL I took on August 19th. Most of the time it's like this. The price is indecisive and then it breaks up or down. If I cut the position at BE, considering the entry price, the RR just doesn't make sense.

author: Jdub
content:
Just caught back up with the chat.

I'd like to give my thought on some of the things said.

Taking signals is not a long term solution. I can promise you that if you solely rely on signals that you will lose in the long term and let me break it down. A great example of this is yesterday and today. Let's say you took the NVDA trade towards the downside yesterday. Most likely you would have sold on the first push towards the downside because you didn't understand the overall context of the trade. This was a higher timeframe thesis laid out in the pre market prep and we talked about this over the weekend as well. If the market is weak we will be look for NVDA towards the downside. And if you didn't understand this, you would have no conviction to hold for the move towards the downside even though there was a very good chance that it would head lower. 

And today for example lets say you missed the trade yesterday and now you got into NVDA this morning. I mentioned that its a completely different setup and that you have to keep a tight stop on this one as its more aggressive since its close to open. Once again if you didn't understand this it would be hard to capture. Price ended up rejecting off PML and reversing. When it came back up I stopped out quickly because I knew that price was most likely going to reverse. 

What I'm trying to say is that the problem with taking signals is that when you lose you end up taking a full R loss and sometimes even more since you will hold past your stop and when your in a winning trade you sell as soon as your in the green. You can't hold because you don't have conviction for yourself since your relying on someone else but yourself.

And that's just the reality of taking signals. That's why we teach to fish for yourselves. You think Neto became profitable by taking signals. He took some of the concepts that we teach and implemented it to his own trading, where he has his own system, strategy and edge in the markets.

author: demchy19
content: Based on how the market moved and the pre-market setup, I was looking to trade scalps, focusing on retests and then targeting the PDH. For NVDA, my strategy was to enter after it broke out of the PMH. On the 1-minute chart, it showed a good displacement and then retested the PMH, where I entered with 6 contracts. I sold 3 contracts at the HOD, 2 contracts at halfway to the PDH, and the last contract once it broke below the PHOD. I didn't expect it to move too far but was aiming for just the PDH. But after it broke the PDH somehow was really weak compare to overall market (QQQ). I don't understand why. But I still saw a good trade.  Even with the AMZN trade, it was an intraday trade, and it didn’t have higher time frame thesis. The final target was the PDH. This is how I understand the situation and Jack’s approach.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
**RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY **

If you’re green for the week, only trade with a small **PORTION** of your profits. For example, if you’re up $1,000, risk just $200-$300. Even if you lose it, you’ll still end the week green. 

If you’re green but don’t have enough profits to play with, **DO NOT TRADE**. 

If you’re red for the week, **DO NOT TRADE**. 

Even if you’re green and have profits to spare, you can always take Friday off. Enjoy a 3-day weekend—it’s never a bad idea. 

Remember, Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you. 

Stick to these rules, and you’ll never end the week on a bad note.

author: asdf
content:
new chat gpt models are out. here's something to take your mind off of friday destruction:
Q: whats the smartest things anyone ever said?

A: 
"The only true wisdom is in knowing you know nothing."
— Socrates
Highlights the importance of humility and acknowledging one's limitations in knowledge.

"The unexamined life is not worth living."
— Socrates
Encourages self-reflection and the pursuit of self-understanding.

"I think, therefore I am."
— René Descartes
Asserts that the act of thinking is proof of one's existence.

"Those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it."
— George Santayana
Emphasizes the importance of learning from history to avoid repeating mistakes.

"We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act but a habit."
— Attributed to Aristotle
Stresses that consistent actions shape our character and achievements.

"The greatest enemy of knowledge is not ignorance, it is the illusion of knowledge."
— Stephen Hawking
Warns against overestimating what we know, which can hinder learning and progress.

"Not everything that can be counted counts, and not everything that counts can be counted."
— William Bruce Cameron (often misattributed to Albert Einstein)
Reminds us that not all valuable things are measurable, and not all measurable things are valuable.

"Be the change that you wish to see in the world."
— Often attributed to Mahatma Gandhi
Encourages personal responsibility in initiating the changes one desires globally.
(Note: Gandhi expressed similar ideas, though not in this exact wording.)

"The only thing necessary for the triumph of evil is for good men to do nothing."
— Often attributed to Edmund Burke
Highlights the importance of taking action against wrongdoing.

"Education is the most powerful weapon which you can use to change the world."
— Nelson Mandela
Emphasizes the transformative power of education in society.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Hey @everyone,

Just a quick heads-up on why trading options on FOMC days isn’t recommended. The market tends to be super volatile, with quick and unpredictable swings that can throw off even the best setups. Options can get especially tricky because premiums can spike or drop suddenly, making it hard to manage risk. It’s often better to wait until the market settles after the announcement, when you can trade with more clarity and less stress. 

Play it safe—there will always be better, more predictable days ahead!

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content:
Maybe you should rethink your PT not stop loss. With 1000$ most probably you shouldn't trade with more than 1 con, with risk 30$ it's already 3% of the account, you don't wanna risk more. 
The stop loss depends on the entry, it might be below retest candle, below breakout candle, below OB if you enter off OB. Backtesting should tell you where to set stop loss for your exact setup.
But you need to take full profit on 2RR or 2,5RR or whatever RR is average in your backtesting and combines with the win rate to make the strategy profitable.

author: demchy19
content:
Hi Lauren,
I'm not a professional trader yet; I'm still learning, but I'll share my thoughts on your question. I don't think this follows the 84% rule. If I understand correctly, the 84% rule applies when you have a plan for entering a stock at a specific key level (e.g., the 5-minute opening candle print), and the stock tries to retest that level but fails and goes lower. The 84% rule comes into play when the stock returns to that key level and breaks through it. At this point, you just watch the price reaction at that level. Typically, with the 84% rule, the stock doesn't retest the key level—it just breaks through and moves in the right direction.
From the picture below, we can see that the stock has retested the same level twice. This can give you a stronger confirmation that the level is well established and could push the stock lower. There are likely many sellers above this level, preventing the stock from going higher, so there's a good chance it will drop. But based on your take profit and stop loss, you seem to be in a good position.

author: demchy19
content: Today, I was trading NVDA and followed the 84% rule. I planned to enter on the 1-minute OB, but it didn’t hold; instead, it went through. Once the stock reclaimed that level, it was a good entry point. I entered the trade, but I think I was a bit too quick and didn’t wait for the proper timing. Fortunately, the trade moved in the right direction.

author: chunky_beast
content:
<@584423632802021376> I’ll keep this as simple as possible, but please what I’m about to say isn’t meant to be gospel. I don’t like leaving questions unanswered if I can help it. 

When you look at the upcoming earnings you obviously expect an uptick in options trading into that particular week volume wise but what will almost always increase is options holders/open interest (OI) into those options. Using AAPL for example we have a large disparity between Call holders vs Put holders for Nov 1 Weekly’s so with this we can estimate that, as of Friday, the market is currently expecting bullish move into earnings. Plus as you get from Earnings Whispers, on the wider options there was a large volume of Feb 21 265C bought on Friday indicating some bull expects a move towards the upside in the longer term worth putting up $12M. With all this you can infer the market a bullish outlook on AAPL. 

A different case is GOOGL where we can see, as of Friday, there isn’t really any high expectations for explosive short term moves based on next weeks options OI. 

There are other equities you can look at with extremely high bullish option sentiment like AMZN and AMD. 

However this is just a gauge, not a crystal ball. Do your own research and ensure that this correlates with your strategy and playbook. Also if you’re gonna trade shorter dated options into earnings this isn’t a swing where you may lose 60-70%. You can easily lose your equity so you have to be comfortable with a 100% risk to capital overnight.

author: Jdub
content:
Its not really about being upset because as a human you are going to have emotions. It's all about what you do with those emotions. Am I upset when I take a bigger loss than expected. Of course but its all about how you respond and what you do after the fact. 

Trading is all a game about probabilities. I had conviction coming into this morning on TSLA for a continuation higher and guess what it didn't work out. I wanted to only risk around $2000 on this trade and ended up losing over $4000. But what happened next, I didn't do anything irrational or went to do something dumb. What I did was wait for another opportunity to setup. 

We ended up getting in SPY/ES which was a solid setup and is still working out as I type this out. 

As for relying on trading as income I didn't do this for the longest time. When I started out and even when I was profitable I was still delivering packages on the side. I would next recommend someone to do this full time without some sort of income coming in. Having to "rely" on trading is going to make it very difficult no matter who you are. 

Hope that makes some more sense.

author: Mike Y
content: I've been studying and reading my MM5 notes and a piece that I wrote from Jdub word for word (I write this so not only myself but for many to connect with) :  Higher Time Frame Thesis: If your so fixated on lower time frame 1 min chart and you always sell on pops and take profits, sure you might make $400-$500 on 1 min time frame, but when HTF trade comes you've always sold on its pop. So when you are in a HTF trade you can not hold for HTF move. You can't take higher profits because you always come in as a scalpers mentality and sell on pops.  If you focus on HTF you will be able to capture more of the move.  But since you have a 1" lower time frame trading mentality-you will never capture higher time frame PROFITS!     I hope this connects as I am totally guilty of this!  Granted if $400 or $500 is all you want, I get it, but we as traders are not here to be limited.  to be restricted, to be reckless of trade by fear.  We are here to Capture, to provide and broaden our discovery to trading.  We can do this and we got this!! Just give it time and be patient!

author: chino
content:
Hey everyone, I wanted to share today’s trade and ask for some advice. I sold at the first white line the moment i saw green, but look how much it dumped in my favor afterward, straight to my final take profit level.

This kind of mistake happens to me all the time. Fear always gets in the way, and I struggle to hold my trades. Today was especially challenging because the price was dancing near my stop loss, which didn’t help my confidence. On top of that, I assumed there wouldn’t be any strong continuation due to the holiday tomorrow and the market being closed.

What’s frustrating is that I still got rewarded despite making this mistake, which makes it harder to break the habit and learn to just HOLD!

Do you guys have any tips on overcoming this fear and holding trades longer? I feel like this is a major hurdle I need to get past.

author: Royal191
content: hi, It’s never a bad idea to lock in profits and end the day green especially on a day when price was choppy and right before a holiday. author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
**__RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY__**

Listen up, because this is crucial. Take a screenshot of this and pin it somewhere visible.

**If you're in the green for the week**, only gamble with a tiny slice of your gains. Say you're up $1,000, then play with just $200-$300. Even if you blow it, you'll still walk away with a profit for the week.

If you're green but your profits are too slim to play with, **DO NOT TRADE.**

**If you're in the red for the week, DO NOT TRADE.**

And even if you're sitting pretty with some profits to burn, consider taking Friday off. An extra day to relax isn't something to sneeze at.

Remember, **__Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you. __**

Follow these rules, and you'll never end the week wishing you hadn't played.

author: Angel (az)
content:
I can't speak for the pros, but here is my personal experience as a beginner - I'm able to hold the trade and not care because I am comfortable with the risk im exposed to at 2 cons total, I am comfortable with it dropping 50% in value overnight in the worst case. And 2 is easier to manage than 1 IMHO. I don't know how you're sizing but it needs to be what you personally are comfortable with mentally at this point in your journey. I am strict with scaling out at the first pop up which gives you realised gains and it's like a cheat code that makes it easier for you psychologically to hold the second one. The Tesla trade this week for example, my first con scale was 300+ realised, so now psychologically, i am okay with my second con going to -300. (I would not wait that long to stop out, but it lets you breathe and gives you a lot of room mentally). Ever since then, I literally try to not even look at the value of my second con or the price, I am just waiting for the HTF target to hit or structure to break. 

If the trade doesn't work: I believe Jack or Tony said you have your stop losses as usual in terms of trades not going your way, but you have to be more comfortable with a larger percentage drawdown. Stop loss is at where key HTF levels and structures are broken

author: Angel (az)
content: Oh and in case 1, if there are no more HTF levels then you start monitoring the chart real time and have to use more discretion/experience when to close out your last trailers. I would probably try to make myself a rule that's more mechanical since I still lack that experience. So probably something like - I must take profit after first bearish candle closes after ATH, or, after first close under an order block after ATH. That way I have a clear exit plan while I hopefully eventually gain that experience

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)

author: demchy19
content:
Usually, red folder news affects the overall market rather than a specific stock. There’s always a possibility that the news will be good, causing the market to go higher, which would also take TSLA higher. On the other hand, if the news is bad, it could bring the entire market down, including TSLA. And sometimes, the news might not affect the market at all.

I’d say trading based on news is a bit like gambling—it’s always a 50/50 chance whether it goes in your direction or against it. Hopefully, that makes sense! 🙂

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
**__RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY__**

Listen up, because this is crucial. Take a screenshot of this and pin it somewhere visible.

**If you're in the green for the week**, only gamble with a tiny slice of your gains. Say you're up $1,000, then play with just $200-$300. Even if you blow it, you'll still walk away with a profit for the week.

If you're green but your profits are too slim to play with, **DO NOT TRADE.**

**If you're in the red for the week, DO NOT TRADE.**

And even if you're sitting pretty with some profits to burn, consider taking Friday off. An extra day to relax isn't something to sneeze at.

Remember, **__Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you. __**

Follow these rules, and you'll never end the week wishing you hadn't played.

hey all, do you always wait for the retest after a breakout, or is there ever a scenario where a breakout is sufficient signal to take a position?  

2nd question - in most of the trades / tutorials / accelerator, we usually take long positions on the B/R of the ORH, and short positions on the B/R of the ORL.  is there ever a case to take a short position on the rejection of the ORH or a long position on a bounce of the ORL?   thanks.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Great second question! 
We typically align our trades with the macro narrative. For example, if the daily/1-hour/15-minute charts are all showing bullish signals, we're looking to enter on a smaller timeframe with a bullish bias. **That's what we consider an A+ setup - when all these timeframes align.** So, we usually aim for the high of the opening range when we're bullish and the low of the opening range when we're bearish. 

However, there can be scenarios where you might see a rejection at the Opening Range High or a bounce off the Opening Range Low, but these would generally need additional confirmation from the broader market context to be considered viable trades. 
Also, we always keep an eye on all relevant key levels like old highs and lows, pre-market levels, and previous day levels, looking for a retest on those levels and waiting for the retest and the eventual reaction. 

Keep an eye on the macro trends to guide your decisions! The trend is your friend!

content:
Yea makes sense 100%. But risk and position size are not the same thing and often confused. 

Like Tony will have a position size of $30k but only risk $4k if the trade goes bad which would be %4 risk if the account if it’s a $100k account but if the trade works that how you get those $50k+ days while only risk a small amount. 

Or Ex : You can put on a trade that’s $1000 in size but only actually risk $100-$200 as the stop loss. I think a lot of people get confused between the position size and the stop loss which is the actual risk in the trade. Hope that makes sense. I was confused by this when starting as well 🙏

author: DWC2016
content:
Yea no problem I think this is a common missunderstanding. I traded crypto for about 6 years so about 80% of the concepts transfer over. 

But yea its a balancing act of the position sizing so you can make money when things works and the actual risk (stop loss) your willing to loss. The stop is the actual risk not the sizing unless you dont follow your stop loss rules then you will doom yourself 😅.

But In futures its a bit more straight forward to set a 20% SL but in options you have to do a bit of math. 

but you have to look at where your stop is and relative to the price of the contract cost if the underlying assest moves xyz percentage then the contract to move roughly the same. These is a video on youtube that explain these things. search " how to set a stop loss based on the underlying asset price" on youtube. 

Also if you type on youtube "how to set a stop loss with IBKR" you should see a few videos that show you. Its fairly simple.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Validate Trendline: Ensure it connects two or more significant swing lows/highs without cutting through candles. Retests confirm validity.
Trendline Break: Redraw if price closes decisively beyond the trendline with strong momentum or volume.
New Structures: Adjust for new formations, like flags or consolidations, as they develop.
Confluence Levels: Prioritize trendlines that align with key support, resistance, or Fibonacci levels.
Timeframe Matters: Short-term trendlines (1-min, 5-min) may require frequent adjustments due to volatility.
Price Behavior: Watch for tight consolidation or "riding" along the trendline as a signal for potential breakout.
Stay Flexible: Update trendlines dynamically based on real-time market changes and dominant structures.

author: Nines
content: This is essentially how you counter trend. Say your in a bearish market and you want to take a short but the next bearish key level is a bit far away and you dont wanna short at the current price because its over sold. You wait for order flow to switch from bearish to bullish on the 1h and 4h and then you take a long until that key level is met. Its crucial that you let a bottom form first and not just try and catch the bottom. Never ever try and predict the bottom u let it form and then play the counter trend

author: BrokeAintNoJoke
content:
Headlines on bloomberg article regarding DeepSeek and AI market. 

“DeepSeek, a Chinese AI startup that’s just over a year old, has stirred awe and consternation in Silicon Valley after demonstrating breakthrough artificial intelligence models that offer comparable performance to the world’s best chatbots at seemingly a fraction of the cost. 

DeepSeek’s emergence may offer a counterpoint to the widespread belief that the future of AI will require ever-increasing amounts of power and energy to develop. 

The app distinguishes itself from other chatbots like OpenAI’s ChatGPT by articulating its reasoning before delivering a response to a prompt. The company claims its R1 release offers performance on par with OpenAI’s latest and has granted license for individuals interested in developing chatbots using the technology to build on it. 

Though not fully detailed by the company, the cost of training and developing DeepSeek’s models appears to be only a fraction of what’s required for OpenAI or Meta Platforms Inc.’s best products. The much better efficiency of the model puts into question the need for vast expenditures of capital to acquire the latest and most powerful AI accelerators from the likes of Nvidia Corp. That also amplifies attention on US export curbs of such advanced semiconductors to China — which were intended to prevent a breakthrough of the sort that DeepSeek appears to represent.”

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
**__RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY__**

Listen up, because this is crucial. Take a screenshot of this and pin it somewhere visible.

**If you're in the green for the week**, only gamble with a tiny slice of your gains. Say you're up $1,000, then play with just $200-$300. Even if you blow it, you'll still walk away with a profit for the week.

If you're green but your profits are too slim to play with, **DO NOT TRADE.**

**If you're in the red for the week, DO NOT TRADE.**

And even if you're sitting pretty with some profits to burn, consider taking Friday off. An extra day to relax isn't something to sneeze at.

Remember, **__Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you. __**

Follow these rules, and you'll never end the week wishing you hadn't played.

content:
Hey Traders,
@everyone

Another fantastic Weekly Recap in the books! Here’s what we covered:

✅ Reviewed this week’s trades
✅ Reviewed mistakes made and how to avoid them in the future
✅ Key lessons to improve our strategies:

- Trade setups: 1' & 5' OR BNR (Opening Range Break & Retest)
- PDH/PDL BNR: Previous Day High/Low Break & Retest
- Key levels: Based on High Timeframe Thesis
- 84% Rule Strategy: a second high-probability entry at the previous BNR point after being stopped out on an initial trade.
- Choosing strike prices for contracts
- Managing stop losses:
🔹 1-BBB (Bar-by-Bar)
🔹 OB (Order Blocks)
🔹 Trailing stops
🔹 Break-even stops
🔹 Hard stops
With the market shifting and volatility on the rise, scalping is becoming the go-to strategy over swing trading. But don’t worry—we’re adapting together!

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Copy of the email which I rec'd from Tony - 

If you’re reading this email, you've already experienced our Accelerator and may have some idea about Traders Lab.

However, I want to take a step back and give you a clear overview of what we’ve built and who this is for.

​

The mission of Traders Lab is to help traders optimize their performance to build a profitable career through tailored education.

Anyone that’s been able to become a profitable trader for a consistent period, it took them at least a few years to get everything right.

Our goal is to cut down those years of trials and tribulations.

author: NickA
content: joined only 1 week ago... Copying them straight up has given me winners and losers.... but you have to handle your own risk/sizing/etc.  Try to make it about the process and evolving away from copying T and J to following along to learn the decision making and flow... easier said than done...  improve scanning technique on multiple symbols and id'ing the key levels with them....  study their pre-market strategy and real-time thought processes and adjustments... it's game changing for me...  seeing them in action helps me modulate my trading frequency and build a better eye for hitting better trades.   Still trying come up with my own actionable trades with better entry/exit plans every day and calming down in general.  Using their actions and comments as a "confluence" but not the only reason to jump in a trade is the pattern I am trying to break.... I am gaining more comfort with the idea of doing it solo. Still so much growth required (and accelerator training) but it's starting to slow down and feel more settled thanks to both T, J, and the community.

author: DarthTrader
content: I base it off the underlying technical level. I do the math on how much I want to risk per contract and take it accordingly. I actually scale into my position so my initial position isn't huge and my losses are smaller as most trades fail in the first few min if they don't go right away. Once the make a new high or low, I actually add to my position (full) then adjust SL as needed. By this time usually we are breaking the highs/low and retesting. If that fails then break even it is. If not we are good.

author: 𝕁𝕒𝕪
content:
Bro, you lost $30k over 5 years. That’s $6k a year. $500’a month. $125 a week. $75 a day.  

Look at it as your 5 year tuition to learn practice and perfect.  

Are you trading to earn a living. Is it a hobby or are you trading solely to get your losses back ? 

Figure out why you’re trading. You can’t make up 5 years in 1 day or 1 week.  Has it been done in a week or a month SURE. But if we get stuck in a mindset we know is wrong yet are only aware of it after we go against it then the losses are gone and there was no tuition. It was essentially gambling.  

I been there, believe me. I’ve blown $35k in less than 2 months.  I feel your pain. A lot of us do.  

If you make $75 a day and shut her down you will get your losses back in time.  
You’re going to have good days and bad obviously but if your RM is there and you’re actually journaling and going over your journal weekly at least it’s going to make it.  

Anyone can make a trade, we have to find trades.  
All Love Bro. Hope you’re able to get a handle on it all and breathe.

author: Nines
content:
some food for thought for you guys; 

scaling out or taking partials are for those who dont trade the bigger picture. reason u never see me scaling out a contract and holding to my tp because it skews my RR significantly. instead of making 6R ill make only 2.5R or lower. then you wonder why your losses are as big as your winners or even bigger. 

with option its tricker because you almost have to scale out because greeks will kill you if you dont, but if you trade futures or forex i recommend you hold to your tp because the days you lose which you will, it wont hurt because your constantly catching 5R trades on the regular. 

im not saying this to bash on anyone who scales out if that works for you and you have success doing that then keep doing it but for me in my experiene i used to see my losers were as big as my winners even tho i was catching 6R trades but when i would scale out at hod or a random level i had marked it skewed with my RR

author: Gabor Nagy
content: Personally I also see size problematic. While backtesting, I am used to scale and therefore have profits or breakeven even if the trade reverses. author: Kam
content:
hey there, while the key levels to take a trade may be different - the qualifying criteria for a break & retest at any level is the same (e.g. clear displacement; strong/weak price action at level, etc). 

using the 1m opening range is usually a more aggressive entry model, so if using the 1m OR we ideally like to look for confluences at that level (orderblocks or an opening play like a gap & go scenario, pre-market level, news, etc). 

Because of the big gap-down today and aggressive continued selling at the open, using a more aggressive entry model on shorts was necessary to try and capture continuation since in most gap-down scenarios we like to look for pops into higher timeframe levels before looking for setups - this generally leads to a higher probability & longer duration trades on the short side.

the best way to get deeper on this to to backtest and log some data to figure out which levels work best for you and on which tickers

also check out Tony's trade review on NVDA today; he goes over why the 1m opening range setup nicely after the gap-down with orderblock & PML confluence

<#1219022089252503632> 

 hope that helps!

author: BrokeAintNoJoke
content:
Part of the risk with holding overnight and over weekend. A war could breakout, terrorist attack, natural disaster, fraud report, etc. 

Only trade with what you’re willing to lose because overnight is a crapshoot. 

Very unfortunate that Moody released the downgrade. I would even guess it was strategically timed as the market has rebounded from earlier lows. We just have to accept it as part of the game. 

Once you have a proven system to trade and confidence from back and forward testing, it is easier to accept the market for what it is, uncertain. 

Your edge in uncertainty is your back and forward tested system with proven probable edge and sound risk management. 

You have to know if we go to war overnight you’re okay with losing whatever is in the market overnight. 

I have lost quite a bit from following alerts and it has made me a better trader.

author: Jose96
content: What i explained was based on the stock itself, but trading the option contracts. Say a stock is at 200 and based on market structure, the stoploss would be a break below 199.8. If you buy calls when the stock is at 200, your stoploss would be a 20cent move. If you buy 10calls, and you assume every will decrease 50$ per 1dollar move of the stock, a 20cent move will equal approx 100usd loss (0.2$ x 50$ x 10cons = 100). It wont be exact becoz of how contracts work, but should be a decent rule of thumb if you buy 1otm contract(40-50delta). If the SL is at 199, thats a 1dollar move, so if you want to only lose around 100usd if you lose the trade, then you would buy 2contracts (1$ x 50$ x 2cons = 100). If you buy the same 10cons for a 1dollar SL, you could lose around 500usd (1$ x 50$ x 10cons = 500). This is just something i use to sort of know how many contracts i’m okay buying for a specific trade opportunity, hope it helps a little

author: Elvis
content: I spent the day to figure out the options buying power so I hope this helps others as well, the contract price does not mater as buying power it’s the stock price that needs to be used to size. Let’s say a contract delta is .5. Tesla price is 345 and nvidia price is 135. The options will move 50 cents on 1 dollar move in the stock price. So if you have a 1% move on nvidia this would be 1.35 dollars so the contract will move about 67 cents and same 1% move on Tesla will be 3.45 dollars so the contract will move 1.72 dollars. So to get same size and risk consistently buying power needs to be measured by stock price. So if your consistent buying power is $1000 that you always need to buy 1000 worth of stock price not contract price to have consistent buying power. Hope others find it helpful, I spent some time doing options calculus haha. But again this depends on delta and that you are always taking the same contract for example always one out the money.

author: GoodVibez
content: At what Price point did you enter your shorts? This is something that I've kept in mind as I've experience a few big hit and have seen some comrades take some massive hits. This one thing... the market is predisposed to go higher. It's nature. We plant seeds so they can grow. Big Institutions want more, not less. So moves down aren't beneficial in the broader picture. I only enter puts in the short-term sense. IF it hits my stop loss or doesn't move towards my target with immediate conviction... I'm out that trade. I also don't leverage as much when entering shorts. Holding shorts for swings is a no-go in my rule book. I wait for big retracements or breakout levels and buy UP for time out. The likelihood your cons recover with longs/calls is higher. So the general consensus...CUT YOUR LOSSES, before the losses become too large for you to recover from. Can't recover if you don't have any funds to recover with. And the longer you wait... the more theta will eat your liquidity.

author: ma
content:
The key to trading options lies in momentum and volatility volume needs to come in.
If you're trading zero-day options and your stock doesn't move in your favor or just consolidates around  the first 10 to 15 minutes, you're likely to lose premium. Even if the move eventually goes your way, unless it's a big and immediate shift, recovering that lost premium is tough.
The best approach Cut your losses quickly.
Options are tricky. That's why traders like Tony prefer trading during the early market hours your trade usually plays out within 5 minutes. You either win or lose, but you avoid time decay, which is an extra loss that many beginners overlook.

Only consider swing trading if you're truly skilled at reading charts and fully understand market conditions. In today’s market, a single news event can invalidate all your technical levels news plays a major role.

author: Sean
content:
I delt with this alot recently. Slowly starting to over come it, but there are a couple things that come to mind. 

One of the bigger things that helped me was changing my goals. I used to focus on profits and losses. Money. Greed and fomo were getting to me. Especially when seeing others prosper. 

I decided to switch to more process focused goals (i.e. Did i follow my plan, respect my stop, respect my take profits...etc).  Doing so , I think, in time the profits will flow. 

Also realizing that is more about probability.  If you take the same trade/setup every day over a long period of time, you'll win out in the end if you have a proven edge in the market. Backtest & journal. Reflect. Have set max loss per trade and max loss per day, and max amount of trades per day. Implement what helps regulate those emotions in other aspects of your life when you feel them arise. For me, shutting things down and walking away and exercising helps settle things down. 

Hope this helps. 

Don't give up. This is a long drawn out battle against ourselves. Master the mind, master the markets.

author: Bhargav
content:
From $500 to $7,876 – My Journey of Patience, Discipline, and Belief

Hey everyone,

I wanted to take a moment to share something close to my heart – not to boast, but to hopefully inspire anyone who's feeling stuck or uncertain in their trading journey.

I joined this trading community about a year and a half ago. Back then, I was just eager to learn, hungry for growth, and like many of you, figuring things out one step at a time. It wasn’t always smooth. There were days filled with doubt, losses that made me question everything, and moments when I wondered if this journey was really for me.

But I kept showing up.

I studied, practiced, journaled my trades, learned from my mistakes, and leaned on this community for support and accountability. Every win and every loss taught me something valuable – not just about trading, but about myself.

In June, I decided to take on a personal challenge: turn $500 into $5,000. Not only did I hit that milestone, but I ended the challenge with a $7,876 account.

Yes, the numbers are exciting – but what I’m truly proud of is the mindset it took to get there: patience, consistency, emotional control, and unwavering belief.

This journey has shown me that it’s not about overnight success – it’s about persistence, process, and trusting that your time will come if you don't give up.

To anyone in this community who’s still grinding, still learning, still waiting for that breakthrough: keep going. You’re closer than you think.

Much love and gratitude to all of you who've been part of this path. The journey is far from over — let’s keep growing, together. Big Thanks to <@717554586843611217> , <@801169196015943731> and all the Mentors here.

– Bhargav

author: Brute7
content:
This was drawn manually before I created the script, and I hope it helps illustrate my experience.

Without R3 marked, many intraday traders might continue holding their positions, expecting further upside. Due to FOMO, they may end up selling only after the price starts dropping. Since I have R1, R2, and R3 plotted, I would sell a few contracts at R1 and the remaining at R2 and R3.

Again, at R1 we saw strong price action, which drove the move all the way to R3 and beyond. This allowed me to re-enter the trade at R1, sell a few contracts at R2, and the rest at R3.

author: Brute7
content:
I was able to complete script for ( Intraday Session Levels (Pre-Mkt, 5min, 15min)) and will only publish after testing it tomorrow. 

Quick glance of this indicator. 

Intraday Session Levels: Pre-Mkt, 5m, 15m (Auto-Reset)
Automatically plot the most important live intraday levels for each trading day—no manual work required!

**What this script does:**
**1. Pre-Market High & Low:**
Tracks and draws the live high and low from 4:00 AM to 9:30 AM ET. These are key reference points as regular trading opens.

**2. First 5-Minute Candle High & Low:**
After 9:35 AM ET, automatically draws horizontal rays for the high and low of the opening 5-minute range (9:30–9:35 AM ET).

**3. First 15-Minute Candle High & Low:**
After 9:45 AM ET, draws rays for the high and low of the initial 15 minutes (9:30–9:45 AM ET), used by many for breakout/reversal setups.

**4. Automatic Cleanup:**
All plotted levels are cleared after 4:00 PM ET, so your chart is always clean for the next session.

**5. Fully Automatic, Live Updates:**
The script updates in real time, resets for each new day, and handles all session times in U.S. Eastern Time.

Hey <@832720687193522227>, I was trying to backtest this, but it only shows for the following trading day levels. Is there any way that you could have these levels shown for the historical data, to help with the backtest and build confidence to use

author: Brute7
content:
<@739730143693766676> Great question! The S/R levels in this script are classic Floor Pivots, which are always calculated from just the previous regular session’s high, low, and close—not by looking for swing points or historical pivots “to the left.” This makes them uniformly spaced and mathematically derived, not price action-based.

This style is perfect for scalping and intraday trading, because it gives you instantly actionable levels that reflect the market structure from the most recent session—great for gaps, mean reversion, and fast trades at key S/R zones right from the open.

There’s a ton of room to customize

You could script additional pivots using longer historical data (multi-day, multi-week, or even price-action “look left” swing pivots).

You can also add as many custom levels or dynamic pivot algorithms as you want, depending on your strategy.

If you want a “look left” style (true swing or historical pivots), that’s a very different type of script—totally possible, but would need to be designed to scan X bars back for true support/resistance.

This one is focused on consistency, speed, and using “yesterday’s map” for today’s trades.

**However, I like your suggestion and I will release v4.0 version to plot the pivots and S/R levels from the last 10 sessions as well.**

author: Brute7
content:
<@1148249290293116990>:
The gap line and label in the script are specifically designed to show the difference between the previous day’s close and the current day’s 9:30am open.
It should not be referencing the previous day’s open; the true “gap” is defined as “current session’s open at 9:30am ET minus previous session’s close.”
If the gap label appears at the wrong price or isn’t updating correctly, it’s usually due to chart timing (e.g., before the new session starts) or a possible visualization glitch, especially on non-intraday timeframes.

After the new session opens at 9:30am ET, the gap label should appear at the current session’s open, showing the value “current open – previous close.”

Just published new version (v4.0) with ON/OFF toggles for all key levels!

What’s New in v4.0:

ON/OFF Switches for Every Level:
Easily toggle the display of each key level (Prev High, Prev Low, Prev Close, Pivot, R1, R2, R3, S1, S2, S3, and Gap) directly from the indicator’s settings panel.

Improved Usability:
Show only the lines and labels you care about—customize your workspace and reduce chart clutter instantly.

No Visual Duplicates:
All objects are managed cleanly: turning a level OFF fully hides its line/label, with no overlap or ghosting.

<@739730143693766676>:
I’m working on adding 10-day key levels to the same indicator script so you can plot historical key levels from the last 10 sessions.
Since this will significantly expand the code and potentially affect chart performance, I’ll publish it as a separate indicator.

author: GoodVibez
content: I consider it every single time. Tony and Jack often mention sizing for zero. It's why strength/momentum is very important. I will often buy an extra week out just to help account for some of the loss due to theta if I see something on HTF, but it doesn't move right away and chops around, but never hits my stop. Yes, buying further out requires a little more of your capitial and puts more of it at risk should the trade move against you w/ lots of strength. Why we have stops though right. But the plus side, is that if you have conviction in your HTF thesis and just feel it needs a little more time to play out, it gives you the ability to hold a trade longer and not have Theta eat as much liquiditiy as fast. This may be beneficial for someone who doesn't have a margin account, has limited funds, and is limited the amount of day trades they can enter w/n a week. They don't have the luxury of opening and closing as many trades.

author: John H
content: I don't think someone else's contract size matters. We have to size to our own accounts and the risk we're willing to take with each trade. For example, your risk might be $100 where Jack & Tony may be at $1000.  So, think in terms of if they bought 50 option contracts, they could only lose $.20 before they hit their max loss. If you wanted $.20 of risk equaling $100 then you could only buy 5 contracts. And, $.20 can happen in a split second. That's why knowing where you are getting out, and sizing appropriately to the number of contracts you can buy is important. Your stop might be at $.50, which would then mean only 2 contracts. Hope this makes sense. At the end of the day, it's not apples to apples to try and compare our trade size to what they are doing.

author: Kam
content:
Hey Viper - this is definitely a loaded question lol.
Hard to answer without seeing more of your data or trading patterns, but here are a few thoughts that might help:

**Plan your levels + scenarios**
If you don’t have a mapped path for where 3R–5R could come from (HTF levels, gaps, reclaim zones), it’s hard to stay in the trade. Build the scenario before you enter.

**Revisit the HOD scale**
If you're trading a small number of cons and scaling at 1R/HOD, you're likely choking the trade by taking a large % off before the trade really gets moving. That said, don’t throw out the HOD rule blindly -  let structure & context guide 

**Mind your strike**
Pay attention to where underlying price is relative to your strike when you hit HOD. A lot of the juice in options comes from delta expansion past the strike - not just tagging it.

**Trailer logic **
Even something simple like bar-by-bar, 9EMA, or last HL break helps you stay in runners. Without a plan, it's easier to flatten early.

**Journaling & Expectations**
Which setups actually run 3-5R+? Even if you scaled out early, make sure you're tracking which trades run. Not all of them do. Also keep in mind that even if the max R is 5R, most likely you won't be able to capture the full 5R due to scaling into pops. I try to aim for 50-60% "R capture" as a metric I track regularly. 

Let me know if you want to walk through a specific trade. feel free throw one up in <#1219021113686888478>  and tag me - happy to dive in.

author: 305 Trader
content:
i listen to a lot of mindset videos in youtube. Today I heard something caused a shift in my thinking. It is to ask this question before entering any trade:

- Where am I wrong? It means, where would the trade get invalidated? Measure it. 
- Am I ready to be wrong and to accept that loss and be ok? 

Also, Tom Hougaard says we have to flip the switch. Start always from thinking you are wrong before taking the trade and how much that would cost. that is reverse thinking that undoes the need to be right before entering.

author: Jose96
content: Hi bro, this is what i do: my stop level is always somewhere where trade is invalidated and my risk is a dollar amount (in my case 100$ is my risk this month), so what i do is i theorically calculate the number of contracts so that when my stoploss hits i lose around 100$, but the level where i would lose 100$ is offesetted to the level that invalidates my trade, that way i can hold the trade until the candle closes and not exceed the 100$ risk amount(most of the time). For example, i enter a call and the level that invalidates my trade is 180, i will size my trade in a way that i will lose 100$ at 179.8 for example (it will depend on the range of the candles of that particular day), but will stop out on a candle close below 180, the extra 20cents is just to not get wicked out at the key level. I’m not experienced but maybe this makes a some sense to you

author: 305 Trader
content: After reading and listening quite a bit on trading mentality, I think I wll keep using a hard stop loss based on a point of invalidation of the trade that I can both afford and that it makes sense to the PA. Mental stop loss, causes pain avoidance and promotes a “hope” which leads to a bloodbath.. We have to think not in on trade but in 50-100 trades. A hard stop loss will keep my losses small while I work on letting my winners ride. Overtime this will lead to a profit factor above 1. This is what matters, not the win rate. If this does not drive my profit factor up (keeping losses small), then I have a problem with my entries and my trade plan that I would need to review but only after my metrics of dozens of trades tell me so.

author: Jdub
content:
This is not an alert service. 

If you want alerts this is not the place to be. 

What matters is the risk amount and thesis. 

If you just copy alerts it doesn't work because you don't understand the thesis and logic behind the trade. 

Even if you get the same entries ultimately if you don't build your own thesis and confidence you won't be able to hold onto the trade if it works out because your reliant on someone else besides your self.

This is what I've noticed Tony and Jack do, and makes a lot of sense. Scale out 25% or 50% at first target, then another 25% and let the rest run until it breaks the trend.

My problem right now is, I only have enough in the account for one to two contracts per day... so I can't really do the 50, 25, 25 option.  Also it would mean I would need to get in at 100% on the get go... and I'm still building the confidence enough to go to 100% max shares off the bat.

author: Jerry
content:
Yeah. Doing your own analysis is essential. Only that way you will get confidence. I simulated different appproaches.
1. No scaling. That gives the best results - best RR and 50% win rate
2. 1 scale. Again tried to find an optimum with simulation where and how many to scale to improve win rate at the price of RR. Managed to do it. Increased to 65% win rate with some combinations but it hit the total profit.
3. 1 scale + runners. Same results as in second approach.

So after all simulations no scaling approach gives the best profit, though much more complicated from mental perspective. 

To the question why Tony manages to have such great result - i see 3 points
1. Much better entry so good RR on HOD
2. Scaling based on good reading of price action like scaling out when several wicks as a sign of weakness
3. Scaling out base on context and not on predefined structure.

But all these points come with experience. We will get there someday

author: Luke
content:
With these trades I scale them purely based on the underlying key levels and not the contract's percentage. How I personally use the contract's percentage to my advantage so avoid unnecessary losses is once i'm in at least 15-20% ish profit (which if you get a move down right away will automatically happen) I don't want to make it a loss so I won't hold to my stop loss. The only time I will hold to my stop loss is if the trade doesn't work out right away. So like at my entry here you can see I wanted to scale 40% at 659, but it didn't hit it and rejected, and my contracts went to around breakeven. If there was any strength after that 10:17 candle I would've stopped out immediately with a super small loss (pretty much breakeven).
As far as the R:R tool goes on the underlying chart. With 0DTEs you can't really base it off that much because they move way differently. Sometimes on the underlying you'll be up 1:1R but the contracts are up almost 2R. One thing I personally will do is if the contracts move to 40% and i'm not at TP1 yet, i'll scale some no matter what because that's like a 2R on the contracts because on these type of trades you should be losing about 15-20% on your losses.
So for your first trade that was up 30%, if I hadn't hit TP 1 yet then I would for sure have stopped out when the contracts hit breakeven, because you can always look to re-enter, so just save yourself from a loss and reevaluate. For your second trade, if it hadn't hit TP 1 yet and the contracts were at 30%, then I would've stopped out breakeven. How I play it is when the contracts are at 40% (2R for the contracts) and price rejects, i'll sell the contracts at 15-20% in profit no matter what so that it doesn't become a red trade.
Those are just some rules i've made for myself over the past weeks of trading thse, hopefully that helps

The thing is that options do not behave like the underlying asset, but only very similarly. However, we do not trade the underlying asset and use the option merely as leverage but we use the underlying as our „road map“. So just put two buttons together. Buy Market 
Sell market 

Don’t Look on the option chart. Buy when the underlying chart tells you to buy and exit when the underlying chart tells you again (by stop loss or take profit)

author: EeveeWinter
content: Part of it is momentum. Tony and Jack always say “scale on pops! Or scale on strength!” That’s because the contracts are juiced in those moments and they provide a nicer market value. Same goes for when a trade reverses. Let’s say the upside is moving slowly and then all of a sudden some red candles form quickly to your stop loss. You’re now cutting your loss into weakness and you’ll feel it more. Today was a perfect example on that first NVDA trade. When Tony cut it, he had to take a bigger loss because that candle that broke structure was big and fast. Bigger loss.

author: healthiswealth
content: I had this same question as well. I was winning, but the losses were twice as big. What I noticed was that I was not scaling out my wins properly. Also, I didn't have trailers. Having trailers are HUGE in order to maximize a win. There have been trades where Tony and JDub have made more from the trailers than they did at the first and second scale outs. So, when we don't scale out properly and have trailers, losses will be greater than the wins because we don't match Tony/JDub's exact winning formula and we "leave meat on the bone", but we do match exactly the formula when they take a loss.

author: Markellwhite16
content: Good question! So this may sound crazy to most but I don’t set profit targets.. I believe traders hurt their PnL when they do so.. our job as traders is to control our emotions and control our risk .. not control or predict the outcome.. so when you set a profit target technically you’re making the assumption that price is gonna stop there… so for me I like to sell into a very strong momentum pushes… when we broke through high day I was still in that full position… I sold at peak high and then from my runners I sold those when we pulled back… I hope that makes sense?? I’m always looking for a continuation so I basically let Market structure. Tell me when to exit my trades.

author: Markellwhite16
content: Absolutely! And for a small account tbh I wouldn’t really focus too much on runners . Every trade you take you should focus on high RR trades and let them play out. Becuse if your scale out you’re not going to make much with 1-2 cons you get me ? You’re better accepting the risk , sitting in your trades , wait until your PT gets hit and sell. And ofc always respect your stop loss! For a small account have a HARD stop . If your stop loss is 10% as soon as you see that hit get out the trade. This will help you long term because your wins will be bigger.

author: 305 Trader
content:
Here is a really sinple method I use as an options trader for scalping,

a)- What is the max loss per trade I am willing to accept today given my account size?

b)- when the set up is forming, I watch where my TPs are, I take a TP1 and also the ultimate, TP1 to me should be 1:2, the ultimate is whatever it can be. Understand this is a super conservative  (but game changer) approach. 

c) - Here is the science, what is the underlying (real stock) $cents size of the most realistic stop loss (not an arbitrary one)??
d)
Formula: 

 c) x contracts number x 0.5 = a). 

Example : Stop loss measures  25 cts of stock value. 
Max loss per trade is $50
Factor is 0.5
So,
25 x 0.5 = 12.5
$50 / 12.5 = 4 contracts. 

That is your contract size to lose $50 as per your measured stop loss (as long as you exit when it says so). 

then, 

Your RvR  ratio to TP1 is 1:2
So,

You have to measure  and find 50 stock value cents to TP1 to enter the trade.

Notice I measure 1:2 RvR to TP1, yes I know this is conservative, but not really cause you are only interested anyways in a  small stop loss, so provided this is what you look for, a RvR to TP1 of 1:2 is realistic and it will occur often. 

Hope this helps, with this method you will be more focused on the  size of your stop loss rather than % of your account to discard set ups that are not winners in what you need: Lower risk

**WHY DIDN'T NVDA WORKOUT TODAY?**

content: I wasn't on the call, but sounds like I probably took the same trade as Tony on the 9:44 candle. author: Kam
content:
Unfortunately the answer is it 'depends' - usually on the type of setup, HTFs , market context etc. On Reclaim trades I take less off (for me less is 10% scale) or none at HOD cos those tend to move aggressively in my favor and I don't want to choke off the trade prematurely. On retests, it depends how much the stock has moved already. If the retest is earlier in the session I'll take less off at HOD etc. 

Keep in mind if trading generally smaller size, each con counts - the math tends to work better if  let our trades run - but def need to backtest and look at your live data to find the answer that works best for you!

author: Jonathan
content:
All losses teach something. If that NVDA trade still showed a 84% rule after losing a good chunck of % why would you enter again? Personally to me, even if the 84%rule shows itself if my loss has reached a point where i can't mentally handle. I do not enter and just wait for tomorrow. So you not entering that 84% trade can help you depending on how you see it or put into perspective. You already took a big L. Could you have handled another L if that 84%failed? 
Its good to disect every single loss trade and why they failed. 
What was your bias on the past trades you took? Calls or puts?  Ask yourself which ones favored you and which ones didn't. Calls,puts,tickers, time frames, even trading days. All those losing trades mean something about your psychology while trading.
